channel.title: QE ArXiv Watch Weekly
channel.description: Weekly AI-synthesized digest of quality engineering research from arXiv. Covering Control Charts, Experimental Design, and Reliability Engineering.

item.title: QE ArXiv Watch: Week of March 16 - March 23, 2026
item.description:
QE ArXiv Watch Weekly — Week ending Mar 23, 2026 (4 papers)

If you’ve ever looked at a control chart and thought, “Sure… but how sure are we about the model behind the chart?”, this week had a satisfying answer: treat your kernel hyperparameters like uncertain inputs, push that uncertainty all the way through KPCA, and let the chart admit what it doesn’t know.

Control charts that come with error bars (and that’s a good thing)

Duma et al. take kernel MSPC and do the thing we’ve all hand-waved: lengthscales, variances, noise terms—those “tuned” kernel settings are epistemic uncertainty, not fixed truth. They start with a deterministic kernel calibration (supervised or unsupervised), then run Bayesian inference over kernel parameters (MCMC flavors like NUTS/HMC included), and propagate posterior draws through kernel PCA to get probabilistic T² and SPE charts.

What you get is practical: not just a point estimate for a statistic, but credible bands on the chart and uncertainty-aware contribution plots. On the Tennessee Eastman benchmark, the posterior-mean charts for a vanilla squared-exponential kernel materially improve detection for several faults (mean CI 0.71 → 0.79; some faults jump a lot). The credible bands stay tight in-control and widen under faults—especially SPE—so you can literally watch “model certainty” degrade when the plant goes off-script.

A nuance worth noting: with ARD-SE kernels, posterior uncertainty shrinks and performance looks almost deterministic. That’s reassuring (the Bayesian machinery doesn’t invent drama when the model is well-identified), but it also hints at a rule of thumb: probabilistic MSPC pays off most when your kernel is underdetermined by the data (short histories, regime changes, messy feature scales).

If you’re maintaining KPCA/KPLS monitoring in production, this is one of the more “ready to try” ideas this week: you can bolt uncertainty propagation onto an existing pipeline, then use band width as a diagnostic for “are we extrapolating?”

Design of experiments is getting more “online” (and more honest about compute)

Two DOE papers land on the same theme: sequential design is great—until it’s too slow. Both attack the speed problem, but from opposite directions.

Zhu et al. stay in classical Bayesian design land, but make it genuinely sequential for physical experiments informed by computer models using the Kennedy–O’Hagan calibration/discrepancy setup. Their main criterion is mutual information between a candidate field run and the eventual predictive outputs, fully marginalized over parameter uncertainty. The nice practitioner detail: a hybrid adds a local “predictive complexity” term (gradients + neighborhood slope variation) so you don’t keep sampling only where the GP is uncertain but the function is boring.

The compute story is the punchline: nested Monte Carlo MI estimates are expensive, so they compress mixtures (whitening + k-means + smart merging) and use fast covariance updates. Reported wall-clock drops are big (e.g., ~901s → ~142s in a toy case; ~2762s → ~435s in JAK–STAT5). If you’ve avoided MI-style design because it melts your laptop, this is a credible path back in.

Strouwen et al. go the other way: amortize sequential Bayesian design into a neural policy (a transformer) trained offline with differentiable simulation through an ODE solver. At runtime, the policy is effectively instantaneous (tens of microseconds). The methodological hook is targeted information gain via an sPCE bound, extended to handle nuisance parameters by marginalization—exactly what we want in real systems where noise scales or subject-specific effects shouldn’t hijack the design. For the DC motor example, they beat an online adaptive Bayesian baseline while being fast enough for tight control loops.

The connection between these DOE papers is bigger than it looks: we’re watching a split between (1) making MI/EIG estimators cheaper and more stable and (2) learning a design policy so you don’t estimate MI at runtime at all. If you run repeated campaigns (bioreactors, pharma PK, accelerated tests), amortization starts to look like a serious engineering play.

Reliability lesson of the week: calibration can’t fix information you threw away

Charpentier et al. give a clean decomposition of proper scoring-rule loss (log loss, Brier, etc.) into parts you can act on. The key identity: when you compress features X into a score S=s(X), your expected loss splits into:

  • miscalibration (reliability) at the score level,
  • grouping / information-loss from compressing X \to S,
  • irreducible uncertainty given X.

Why you should care: post-hoc recalibration can drive miscalibration down, but it cannot recover grouping loss. So a perfectly calibrated but overly-coarse risk score can still be predictively poor—and no amount of isotonic regression will save it.

They also explain a frustration many of us have seen: ensembles/stacking can break calibration in surprising ways. Empirically (German Credit split), recalibration helps a GLM a lot on log-loss but hurts a random forest; averaging/stacking can degrade after recalibration. The decomposition makes that feel less mysterious: you’re changing the sigma-algebra you’re living in, and some “fixes” only operate within that information level.

Forward look

We’re inching toward QE workflows where models admit uncertainty explicitly: charts with credible bands, designs that adapt in real time, and forecast evaluations that separate “bad calibration” from “you collapsed the signal.” The question to chew on: in your environment, where are we currently treating epistemic uncertainty as if it were noise—and what decisions would change if we stopped?

Featured Papers This Week

  • Duma et al.: Probabilistic multivariate statistical process control via kernel parameter uncertainty propagation (Control Charts)
  • Zhu et al.: Sequential Bayesian Experimental Design for Prediction in Physical Experiments Informed by Computer Models (Experimental Design)
  • Strouwen et al.: Deep Adaptive Model-Based Design of Experiments (Experimental Design)
  • Charpentier et al.: Decomposing Probabilistic Scores: Reliability, Information Loss and Uncertainty (Reliability)

Explore More: Visit the QE ArXiv Watch Dashboard to browse all papers with AI summaries, interactive filtering, and paper chat.

This digest is automatically generated every Monday. Questions or feedback? Open an issue on our GitHub repository.
